text.title: Stakeholder Analysis — 6-Step Framework
text.summary: Identifies, characterizes, maps, and engages all parties who affect or are affected by a change initiative.

rule.statement: List every party who is affected by, has influence over, must approve, will implement, or will use the solution.
rule.rationale: Missing a stakeholder early guarantees conflicting or incomplete requirements arriving late.

list.item: End User — direct solution users (employees, customers)
Sponsor — provides resources or authority (executive, budget owner)
Domain SME — subject matter expert (process owners, specialists)
Tester — validates solution (QA team, UAT participants)

rule.statement: Document influence, impact, attitude, knowledge, needs, and communication style for each identified stakeholder.
rule.rationale: Without per-stakeholder attributes, engagement is generic and misses resistance early.

rule.statement: Place stakeholders on a 2x2 power-interest grid: Manage Closely (high/high), Keep Satisfied (high/low), Keep Informed (low/high), Monitor (low/low).
rule.rationale: Grid quadrant determines engagement cadence and channel; without this, effort is spread evenly across unequal stakeholders.

rule.statement: For each key stakeholder, record specific needs, concerns, success criteria, and current pain points.
rule.rationale: Stated needs alone are insufficient; unstated concerns drive resistance if not surfaced early.

rule.statement: Assign an engagement approach (Active/Consult/Inform), frequency, and method to each stakeholder based on their grid quadrant.
rule.rationale: A single plan for all stakeholders wastes sponsor time and under-invests in high-impact resistors.

section.title: Step 6: Manage Relationships Continuously

rule.statement: Treat stakeholder analysis as a living activity: re-run the salience classifier after org events (reorg, leadership change, regulatory letter).
rule.rationale: BABOK 3 explicitly calls stakeholder analysis a continuous activity; static artifacts go stale in 2-6 weeks.

section.p: Conflict resolution sequence: understand each position, find common ground, identify trade-offs, escalate to sponsor if needed, document decision with rationale.

example.note: BA activity responsibility assignments using RACI (R=Responsible, A=Accountable, C=Consulted, I=Informed).
example.code: | Activity | Sponsor | SME | User | BA | |-------------------------|---------|-----|------|----| | Define requirements | A | C | R | R | | Approve requirements | A | C | I | R | | Validate solution | A | C | R | R | | Accept delivery | A | C | R | I |

text.reference: Register table for tracking all stakeholders with classifications
Per-stakeholder profile template with needs, concerns, and engagement history
